topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in his inbox like a lead weight, instantly chilling the lukewarm coffee in his mug. *Event cancelled.* A single, stark sentence that echoed in the silence of his apartment. He stared at the screen, the fluorescent light reflecting dully off his eyes. His jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath his skin. He didn't move for a full minute, just the slow, deliberate clenching of his fists until his knuckles were white. The celebratory atmosphere he had meticulously constructed in his head—the laughter, the flashing lights, the genuine affection—evaporated, leaving behind a hollow, cold space. He finally pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping harshly against the hardwood floor. The sound felt too loud in the quiet room. He walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound, heavy inertia settle deep in his chest.

[story 2]

"No." The word was barely a rasp, swallowed by the sterile air of the room. He reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly under the intensity of his focus. The realization hit him not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, sickening descent. His shoulders slumped, the tension that had been coiled tight for weeks finally unraveling into a heavy, defeated sag. He ran a hand through his hair, the gesture jerky and restless. He paced the length of the living room, his footsteps echoing unevenly on the floorboards. Every corner seemed to hold the ghost of the planned celebration, now just an empty, mocking space. A deep sigh escaped him, a sound devoid of relief, heavy with unspoken disappointment.

[story 3]

He didn't look up when the notification popped up. He simply let his gaze drift over the screen, registering the text with a detached, almost painful clarity. The air seemed to thicken around him, pressing down on his lungs. A low hum of dissatisfaction vibrated beneath his ribs. He picked up the discarded wrapping paper from the table, turning the crinkled paper over and over in his fingers, the texture rough against his skin. His movements were slow, weighted, as if moving through deep water. When he finally dropped the paper, the sound was muted, absorbed by the oppressive quiet. He leaned his head back against the cool plaster of the wall, closing his eyes, feeling the weight of everything uncelebrated pressing down on him.
